1: Isaiah 33:1 opens the sixth and final woe of the woe-series (Isaiah 28:1; Isaiah 29:1, 15; Isaiah 30:1; Isaiah 31:1; Isaiah 33:1), now directed not at Judah but at the unnamed destroyer: 'hoi shoded velo shudad uboghed lo bagad bo, woe to you destroyer who has not been destroyed, betrayer who has not been betrayed.' The unidentified destroyer is almost certainly Assyria — the empire that has devastated nation after nation without itself facing reciprocal destruction. The antiphonic structure: shaded/shudad (destroyer/destroyed) and boghed/bugad (betrayer/betrayed) — four words sharing the same consonantal frame, the action and its reversal. 'When you finish destroying, you yourself will be destroyed' (kikallotekha shadod tushad, when you complete destroying, you will be destroyed) — divine justice operates on the lex talionis of cosmic proportion: the destroyer receives the full measure he gave.
2: 'LORD, be gracious to us; we wait for you. Be our arm of strength every morning, our salvation in the time of trouble' — the community prayer that follows the pronouncement of judgment on the destroyer. 'YHWH channenu lekha qivvinu, LORD be gracious to us; we wait/hope for you.' The qivvah (waiting/hoping) is the signature posture of the faithful remnant throughout the Isaiah Apocalypse (Isaiah 25:9; Isaiah 26:8; Isaiah 30:18). 'Their arm every morning' (zero'am labbeqarim, be our arm in the mornings) — 'our arm' here is a reversal of Isaiah 40:10 ('his mighty arm will rule for him'); YHWH acts as the people's arm, their instrument of strength. 'Every morning' (labbeqarim, in the mornings) = the daily renewal of divine protection, each morning a new act of grace. 'Our salvation in the time of trouble' (af-yeshu'atenu be'et tsarah, also our salvation in the time of distress) = the Psalms 46:1 vocabulary: 'God is our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble.'
3: 'At the uproar of your voice the peoples flee; when you rise up, the nations scatter' — the divine-warrior battle-cry: 'miqqol hammon neddu ammim, at the sound of the tumult/uproar the peoples fled.' The hmom (tumult/uproar = the divine battle-roar, the same as the 'roar of many waters' in Isaiah 17:12-13) scattered the nations (amm). 'Miroom'ekha yippotsetshu goyim, when you arise the nations will be scattered' (yippotsetshu from puts = to scatter/be dispersed). The imagery: YHWH 'rising up' (romem, the exaltation of the king to his throne in battle) triggers the enemy's flight. This is the divine-warrior language of Psalms 68:1: 'May God arise, may his enemies be scattered; may his foes flee before him.'
4: 'Your spoil will be gathered as the locust swarm strips a field; like grasshoppers leaping on it, people will rush over it' — the reversal of Assyrian plunder: 've'usaf shalalkhem aseph hechasiyl, your spoil will be gathered as the locust gathers.' The heChaSiyl (the species-of-locust = the devouring locust that strips everything) and arbeh (the swarming locust/grasshopper = the locust in its mature swarming stage, the same arbeh as Exodus 10:4-19's plague of locusts). The Assyrian army that came to plunder Judah (like a locust swarm, consuming everything) has its own plunder stripped away like a field after a locust swarm. The locust-like speed: 'mishaq hashaq bo, like grasshoppers leaping upon it, men will rush over it.' The counter-plundering is as swift and thorough as the original Assyrian devastation.
5: 'The LORD is exalted, for he dwells on high; he has filled Zion with justice and righteousness' — the declaration of YHWH's enthronement after the enemy's defeat: 'nisgav YHWH ki shokhen marom, exalted is YHWH for he dwells on high.' Nisgav (from sagav = to be high/lofty/inaccessible — the same root as Isaiah 2:11's bringing low what is lofty: YHWH's exaltation is the obverse of the proud's humiliation). 'He has filled Zion with justice and righteousness' (mille Tsiyon mishpat utsedaqah, he has filled Zion with justice and righteousness). The filling of Zion with mishpat and tsedaqah echoes Isaiah 1:21-27 (the restoration of Zion as 'City of Righteousness') and Isaiah 32:1 (the messianic king who reigns in righteousness). The defeat of the enemy produces the filling of Zion with YHWH's covenant attributes.
6: 'He will be the stability of your times — an abundance of salvation, wisdom, and knowledge; the fear of the LORD is his treasure' — the six attributes of the messianic era: 'vehayah emunath ittekha, and he will be the faithfulness of your times.' Emunath (faithfulness/stability, from aman = to be firm/reliable = the root of 'Amen') = YHWH as the reliable foundation of the era. 'An abundance of salvation, wisdom, and knowledge' (cheshen yeshuot, chokhmah, vada'at, riches of salvation, wisdom, and knowledge). The three wisdom-attributes: yeshuot (salvations/deliverances), chokhmah (wisdom = the practical skill of right living), da'at (knowledge = the covenant-intimacy of relationship with YHWH). 'The fear of the LORD is his treasure' (yirat YHWH hi' otsaro, the fear of YHWH is his treasure) — the fear-of-YHWH as the supreme wealth (Proverbs 1:7: 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom'; Job 28:28: 'the fear of the Lord — that is wisdom').
7: 'Look — their brave warriors cry out in the streets; the envoys of peace are weeping bitterly' — the crisis scene before YHWH's decisive intervention: 'hen er'elam tsa'aqah chutsa, look, the arielim cry out in the street.' The erielam (their arielim = the 'lions of God' = the heroes, the same Ariel-root as Jerusalem in Isaiah 29:1) = the bravest warriors. Even the mighty warriors are crying out (tsa'aqah, the desperate cry for help). 'The envoys of peace weep bitterly' (malachey shalom mar yivkayun, the messengers of peace weep bitterly) — the diplomatic corps sent to negotiate peace (the envoys to Assyria, or perhaps Sennacherib's own peace-terms envoys) now weeping. The failed negotiations: the 'envoys of peace' = Hezekiah's delegation or the Assyrian negotiators whose terms were refused.
8: 'The highways are desolate, all travel has ceased; he has broken the covenant, he has despised the cities, he has no regard for human life' — the breakdown of civilization under Assyrian pressure: 'nashammu mesillot, the highways are desolate.' The mesillot (raised highways/main roads) = the arterial infrastructure of commerce and communication. 'Travel has ceased' (shavat over orach, the wayfarer/traveler has ceased) — commerce, pilgrimage, and communication all stopped. 'He has broken the covenant' (hefir berit, he has annulled the covenant) = the ANE suzerainty treaty violation: Assyria's protocol included sworn treaties with vassal states, and breaking these oaths was a capital offense under ANE law (cf. Ezekiel 17:15-19 on the covenant-breaking of a king who went to Egypt for help). 'No regard for human life' (lo chashav enosh, he has not valued a human being) = the Assyrian dehumanization of defeated populations.
9: 'The earth mourns and fades; Lebanon withers in shame; the Sharon plain is like a desert; Bashan and Carmel are stripping off their leaves' — the mourning of the great fertile landscapes: 'aval umlelah erets, the earth mourns and languishes' (the same pair as 24:4 in the Isaiah Apocalypse). The four geographic symbols of the land's fertility: Lebanon (the cedar forests of the north = the symbol of majestic natural beauty, 1 Kings 5:6-10; Psalms 92:12); the Sharon plain (the coastal plain between Carmel and Joppa = the fertile grain-growing region of the coastal plain; Isaiah 35:2 reverses this); Bashan (the rich pasturelands east of the Sea of Galilee = the prime cattle-grazing region, Psalms 22:12; Amos 4:1); Carmel (the garden-mountain of the north coast, the same Carmel as Elijah's contest in 1 Kgs 18). All four 'stripping their leaves' = the comprehensive ecological mourning before YHWH's intervention.
10: 'Now I will rise, declares the LORD; now I will be exalted; now I will be lifted up' — the divine first-person declaration of decisive action: 'attah aqum, now I will rise.' Three synonymous verbs of divine exaltation: aqum (I will rise = the posture of the warrior/judge standing for action), aromam (I will exalt myself = raise myself to full height), etnaase (I will be lifted up = the technical word for the royal elevation, the same word as the 'lifted up' of Isaiah 6:1: 'I saw the LORD seated on a throne, high and lifted up'). The triple 'now' (attah...attah...attah) = the urgency and decisiveness of the moment. After the mourning of Isaiah 33:7-9, YHWH's standing up is the pivot point of the chapter.
11: 'You conceive chaff; you give birth to stubble; your own breath, like fire, will consume you' — the Assyrian's self-destruction by their own schemes: 'tagru chashash teledhu kash, you conceive chaff, you give birth to stubble.' The biological metaphor of pregnancy applied to their schemes: what they conceived (the plan to destroy Judah) is chaff (chashash = the light chaff of threshing) and what they give birth to is kash (stubble = the dry stalks after harvest, equally worthless). 'Your own breath, like fire, will consume you' (ruchakem esh tokkelkhem, your own spirit/breath, fire, will eat you) — the Assyrian's own power (their ruach = their spirit/breath/military force) will be the fire that destroys them. The self-consuming fire of judgment: they are burned by their own breath. Cf. Isaiah 30:33: the breath of YHWH kindles the Topheth fire.
12: 'The peoples will be burned like limestone; like cut thornbushes they will be set ablaze in the fire' — the comprehensive burning of the enemy: 'vehayu ammim misrefot sid, and peoples will be burnings of lime' (misrefot sid = lime-burnings = the process of burning limestone to produce quicklime). The burning of limestone: limestone was heated in kilns to produce quicklime (calcium oxide), used as mortar and whitewash. The process required sustained intense heat until the stone was completely consumed. 'Like cut thornbushes set ablaze in fire' (kemotsats kotsim baesh yitsatsetshu, like the cutting of thorns, in fire they will burn each other). The thornbush-fire = the same thorns-and-briers judgment-vegetation (Isaiah 9:18: 'thorns and briers will be set on fire') burning rapidly and completely. Both images: complete, quick consumption leaving nothing.
13: 'Hear, you who are far away, what I have done; and you who are near, acknowledge my power' — the universal proclamation after the defeat of the enemy: 'shime'u rechoqim asher asiti, hear, O far-away ones, what I have done.' The 'far away' (rechoqim) and 'near' (qerovim) together = the full geographic scope of YHWH's audience. This is the proclamation to the nations of what YHWH did at Zion — the Sennacherib crisis becomes a demonstration of divine power for the entire world. 'Acknowledge my power' (de'u gevurati, know/acknowledge my might) — yada (know/acknowledge) as the recognition formula: the nations will come to know YHWH through his demonstration of power. Ezek 36-39 uses the same 'then they will know that I am YHWH' formula 50+ times.
14: 'The sinners in Zion are terrified; trembling has seized the godless: Who among us can live with the devouring fire? Who among us can dwell in perpetual flames?' — the terror of sinners before the holy God enthroned in Zion: 'pachadu beTsiyon chatta'im, in Zion the sinners are terrified' — the same Zion that is the refuge of the righteous is the place of terror for the sinners within it. The two questions: 'mi yagur lanu esh okhelah? mi yagur lanu moqdei olam? who can dwell with devouring fire? who can dwell with everlasting flames?' The 'devouring fire' (esh okhelah) and 'everlasting flames' (moqdei olam) = YHWH himself, who is a 'consuming fire' (Deuteronomy 4:24; Isaiah 9:3; Hebrews 12:29: 'our God is a consuming fire'). The theological problem of the holy God dwelling in Zion: his holiness consumes. The answer (Isaiah 33:15-16) is the entrance liturgy — the righteous character that can approach the Holy One.
15: 'The one who walks righteously and speaks honestly, who refuses unjust gain, who shakes his hands free of bribes, who stops his ears from plots of bloodshed, and shuts his eyes from looking on evil' — the entrance liturgy answering 'who can dwell with the devouring fire?' Six righteous qualities: (1) 'holek tsedaqot, walking righteously' (lifestyle); (2) 'dover mesharim, speaking uprightly' (speech); (3) 'mo'es bevetsa maashaquot, rejecting unjust gain' (economic integrity); (4) 'no'er kappaiv mittmokh bashochad, shaking his hands from holding bribes' (judicial integrity — the bribe falling from the palm like a rejected object); (5) 'ottem ozno mishmoa damim, stopping his ear from hearing blood-plots' (refusal to participate in murderous scheming); (6) 'ottsem einaiv mer'ot bera, shutting his eyes from seeing evil' (deliberate avoidance of evil). Psalm 15 and Isaiah 24:3-6 are the closest parallel entrance-liturgies: 'Who may ascend the mountain of the LORD? Who may stand in his holy place? The one who has clean hands and a pure heart...'
16: 'That person will dwell on the heights; his refuge will be a fortress of rocks. His bread will be provided; his water will be reliable' — the reward of the righteous character described in Isaiah 33:15: 'hu meromim yishkon, he will dwell in the heights' (meromim = the high places = the place of YHWH's dwelling, Isaiah 33:5: YHWH dwells on high). The righteous person inhabits the same heights as YHWH himself — access to the divine presence is the supreme privilege. 'His refuge will be a fortress of rocks' (misgav sela'im mesado, a stronghold of rocks is his refuge) = the rock-fortress imagery of Psalms 18:2 and Isaiah 27:5: 'In the day of trouble he will keep me safe in his dwelling; he will hide me in the shelter of his sacred tent.' 'His bread will be provided' (lachmo nitan, his bread will be given) — divine provision for daily needs. 'His water will be reliable' (meimav ne'emanim, his waters are faithful/trustworthy) — the covenant-faithfulness attribute (ne'eman from aman = reliable) applied to water supply.
17: 'Your eyes will see the king in his beauty; they will look out over a land that stretches far away' — the eschatological vision of the messianic king: 'melek beyofyo tir'einah einekha, a king in his beauty your eyes will see.' The melek beyofyo (a king in his beauty = the ideal king of 32:1 who reigns in righteousness, now seen in the fullness of his beauty). This is the vision promised to the righteous person who can 'dwell on the heights' (Isaiah 33:16) — they see the king directly. 'They will look out over a land that stretches far away' (tir'einah erets merhaqim, they will see a land of far distances) = the full extent of the promised land, without the limitation of siege or Assyrian occupation. The contrast: during the Assyrian crisis, the king was in distress and the land was besieged; now the king is beautiful and the land is expansive.
18: 'Your heart will recall the terror: Where is the scribe? Where is the one who tallied the tribute? Where is the one who counted the towers?' — the post-crisis memory of the Assyrian bureaucracy: 'libbekha yehgeh emah, your heart will meditate on the terror.' The recalling of the Assyrian administrative apparatus that managed conquered territories: 'where is the scribe?' (sofer = the Assyrian accountant/tax assessor who calculated tribute) — documented in Assyrian annals as the first administrative step after conquest; 'where is the one who weighed?' (shoqel = the one who weighed tribute in silver — the Assyrian system of exacting tribute by weight of precious metals); 'where is the one who counted the towers?' (sofer et-hamigdalim = the military engineer who surveyed the city's defenses to plan the siege). All three are gone — the terrifying Assyrian bureaucracy that preceded conquest has been removed. The memory of terror heightens the joy of deliverance.
19: 'You will no longer see that fierce people — a people of speech too deep to make out, of a strange tongue that you cannot understand' — the removal of the incomprehensible enemy: 'et-am noz lo tir'eh, the fierce people you will not see anymore.' The am noz (the fierce/bold people = the Assyrian army; noz from nuz = to flee/be bold?) — no longer visible on Judah's horizon. 'A people of speech too deep to make out' (am ammoq safah, a people deep of lip = whose speech is too deep/difficult to fathom). 'Of a strange tongue that you cannot understand' (nilaz lashon ein bina, of a derided/stammering tongue, without understanding). The incomprehensible Assyrian speech (already referenced in Isaiah 28:11: 'by people of strange lips and a foreign tongue') that served as judgment (1 Corinthians 14:21) is now gone. The community is no longer subjected to the terrifying foreign-language demands of conquerors.
20: 'Look upon Zion, the city of our appointed festivals! Your eyes will see Jerusalem — a dwelling at peace, a tent that will never be taken down, whose stakes will never be pulled up, and none of whose ropes will ever be broken' — the permanent Zion tent: 'chazeh Tsiyon qiryat moadenu, look upon Zion, the city of our appointed assemblies.' The moadenu (our appointed times = the three annual pilgrimage festivals of Exodus 23:14-17: Passover, Weeks/Shavuot, Tabernacles/Sukkot). Jerusalem as the festival city — the place to which all Israel gathered for covenant renewal. 'A tent that will never be taken down' (ohel bilthi-yen, a tent that will not be lifted/removed) — the Tabernacle/tent imagery applied to Jerusalem: the city is like the Tabernacle (the mobile sanctuary of the wilderness) but now permanent. The three covenant-permanence markers: stakes never pulled up, ropes never broken = the impermanent tent becomes permanently established.
21: 'But there the LORD in his glory will be for us a place of broad rivers and streams — where no warship with oars will sail and no mighty vessel will pass through' — YHWH as the city's waterway: 'ki im-sham adir YHWH lanu, for there the majestic YHWH will be for us.' YHWH himself is the 'broad rivers and streams' (yeor rechav yadaim, the broad rivers, wide of hands = the wide, many-channeled rivers). Jerusalem had no significant river — the Kidron was a seasonal wadi. YHWH's presence substitutes for the strategic advantage of river-based cities (Babylon on the Euphrates; Nineveh on the Tigris; Thebes on the Nile). 'Where no warship with oars will sail' (lo-telek bo oniyet shayyit, a vessel of oars will not go there) — the absence of enemy fleets despite being on 'rivers.' YHWH's rivers have the strategic advantage of rivers without the vulnerability to naval attack.
22: 'For the LORD is our judge, the LORD is our lawgiver, the LORD is our king — he will save us' — the triple divine governance declaration: 'ki YHWH shofetenu, for YHWH is our judge.' Three constitutional roles of ancient governance (judge, lawgiver, king) all collapsed into YHWH alone: shofetenu (our judge = the judicial role, as in the time of the judges before the monarchy); mechqqenu (our lawgiver = the legislative role, from chaqaq = to engrave/decree); malkenu (our king = the executive/military role). The three functions that human institutions divided between judges, scribes, and monarchs are reunified in YHWH. 'He will save us' (hu yoshiene, he will save us) — the three-role declaration ends in simple salvation. This echoes the Shema theology: YHWH alone is Israel's true ruler, and his rule produces salvation.
23: 'Your rigging hangs slack; the mast cannot be steadied; no sail can be spread. Then a great plunder will be divided — even the lame will carry off their share of the spoil' — the ship-metaphor applied to the enemy: 'nitashu chaveyleikh, your ropes hang slack' (chaveyleikh = your ropes/rigging). The ship under divine judgment: the mast unstable, the sail unable to spread = a vessel completely disabled. Then the reversal: 'az challaq shallal marbeh, then a great plunder will be divided.' The disabled enemy ship is plundered — and even 'the lame will carry off their share of the spoil' (pissechim yivozzu baz, the lame/crippled will divide the plunder). The lame plundering enemy soldiers is the extreme reversal of conventional military logic — the most vulnerable members of the covenant community participate in the victory-plunder. 2 Sam 5:8 records David's original slur: 'The blind and lame will not enter the palace' — now the lame carry the plunder.
24: 'And no inhabitant will say, I am sick; the people who dwell there will have their iniquity forgiven' — the twin blessings of the eschatological Zion: (1) 'velo yomar shaken chaliti, no inhabitant will say, I am sick.' The cessation of illness = the healing of the community that was spiritually sick (Isaiah 1:5-6: 'the whole head is sick, the whole heart faint; from the sole of your foot to the top of your head there is no soundness'). The eschatological community is physically and spiritually whole. (2) 'Ha'am hayoshev bah nessa avono, the people dwelling there will have their iniquity carried/forgiven' — nessa (from nasa = to lift up/carry away = the standard forgiveness-vocabulary: Genesis 50:17; Exodus 10:17; Numbers 14:19; Micah 7:18: 'Who is a God like you, who pardons sin and forgives?'). The comprehensive restoration: no sickness and no unforgiven sin. Revelation 21:4 and 22:3 complete this: no more death, mourning, crying, pain, no more curse.
